chief_complaint: I have burning when I pee.

note.soap:
S:
The patient is a 34-year-old female presenting with a 3-day history of burning sensation during urination. She reports associated fever but denies flank pain or any vaginal discharge. No medication allergies are noted.

O:
No physical exam or vital signs available due to phone-only evaluation.

A:
 - Working diagnosis (most likely): Acute uncomplicated urinary tract infection (cystitis)
 - Differential:
  1. Pyelonephritis (less likely given absence of flank pain)
  2. Vaginitis or sexually transmitted infection (less likely given no discharge)
  3. Urethritis
  4. Interstitial cystitis (less likely given fever and acute onset)
 - Red flags considered: Fever present, no flank pain; pregnancy status noted for treatment considerations.

P:
 - Disposition: Outpatient management with close follow-up
 - Plan: Empiric antibiotic therapy appropriate for uncomplicated UTI in a female patient. Given the patient’s pregnancy status, antibiotic choice should be safe in pregnancy. Recommended regimen; reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated.
 - Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care if symptoms worsen, develop flank pain, persistent high fever, or inability to tolerate oral intake. Follow-up if no improvement within 48-72 hours or sooner if symptoms escalate.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: IDSA Guideline: Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis
IDSA Guideline: Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis
